title: Friedrichshain - Quick Summary
items: **Friedrichshain is Berlin's young, scruffy, east-side district, just across the Spree from Kreuzberg.** It is not about grand sights. It is street art, cheap eats, clubs, a big park and a monumental socialist boulevard, all in one walkable area east of the city centre.

**Its headline sight is the East Side Gallery, the longest surviving stretch of the Berlin Wall (1.3 km) covered in over 100 murals.** It runs along the river near Warschauer Straße and Ostbahnhof, it is free and always open. Go early: it is packed and hard to photograph by late morning.

**RAW-Gelände is the district's raw nightlife yard**, a former railway repair works off Revaler Straße full of bars, clubs, street art and a Sunday flea market. Important for 2026: it is shrinking under a redevelopment fight and several venues faced operating bans in early 2026, so check what is still open before you go.

**The everyday heart is Boxhagener Platz ('Boxi') and Simon-Dach-Straße**, a grid of cafés, bars and restored Art Nouveau buildings. The square has a Saturday farmers market and a relaxed Sunday flea market from about 10:00 to 18:00, quieter than Mauerpark.

**For green space and history, use Volkspark Friedrichshain and Karl-Marx-Allee.** The Volkspark is Berlin's oldest public park (1848), with the Märchenbrunnen fairy-tale fountain and rubble hills you can climb. Karl-Marx-Allee is the wide 1950s socialist boulevard leading to the twin towers of Frankfurter Tor.

**Getting there is easy.** Warschauer Straße (U1, U3, S-Bahn and trams) is the main gateway and puts you next to the East Side Gallery and RAW. Frankfurter Tor and Weberwiese (U5) drop you on Karl-Marx-Allee, and Ostkreuz is the big S-Bahn interchange on the district's edge.
